title: Te Pūtaiao o Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku | The Science of the Mangakōtukutuku Stream
subject: Science
culturalContext: This lesson is grounded in the story of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, the stream that flows through our rohe and gives our college its name. According to Ngāti Kahungunu pūrākau, the stream was named for the kotukutuku (tree fuchsia) that grew along its banks, providing habitat for native birds and medicinal resources for our tūpuna. Students will explore how our ancestors understood the stream's ecosystem through mātauranga Māori and connect this to modern scientific understanding of freshwater health.
learningObjectives: Measure and analyze at least three water quality parameters (pH, turbidity, macroinvertebrate diversity) using both scientific instruments and mātauranga Māori indicators
Compare and contrast traditional Ngāti Kahungunu knowledge of stream health with contemporary scientific monitoring methods through a structured Venn diagram
Design and propose one practical action to improve the health of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku based on both mātauranga and scientific evidence
Communicate findings about the stream's health using appropriate scientific terminology and te reo Māori environmental vocabulary

activities.title: Whakawhanaungatanga ki Te Awa
activities.description: Begin at the streamside with a karakia and introduction to the pūrākau of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku. Students make observational drawings of the stream environment, noting both natural features and human impacts, while learning traditional names for stream components (wai, köawa, paru, etc.).

activities.title: He Tātari Wai - Water Testing Mahi
activities.description: Using water testing kits, students measure pH, nitrate levels, and turbidity at three points along the stream. Simultaneously, they apply mātauranga indicators: clarity for drinking (wai māori), presence of certain plants like watercress (wāteri) as health indicators, and观察 stream bank erosion patterns.

activities.title: Ngā Kōura me ngā Ngarara Wai - Macroinvertebrate Investigation
activities.description: Students use kick-net sampling methods to collect macroinvertebrates from the stream, identifying species diversity and abundance. They compare findings with traditional knowledge about kōura (freshwater crayfish) and other species as indicators of water quality, learning how tūpuna monitored these species for ecosystem health.

activities.title: Te Hōtaka Whakapai Awa - Stream Restoration Design
activities.description: In groups, students analyze their collected data to assess the stream's health. They design a restoration proposal that combines scientific approaches (riparian planting, sediment traps) with traditional practices (respecting tapu areas, seasonal harvesting practices) to improve the stream ecosystem.

resources: Local kaumātua Hōri Waititi (expert in Ngāti Kahungunu environmental knowledge)
Hawke's Bay Regional Council freshwater monitoring kits and guidelines
NIWA's 'Stream Health Monitoring and Assessment Kit' resources
Book: 'Ngāi Tūhoe - Te Urewera: Mātauranga Māori and Freshwater Management'

tasks: Completed stream health data table with scientific measurements and mātauranga observations
Venn diagram comparing traditional and scientific indicators of stream health
Group presentation of stream restoration proposal with justification from both knowledge systems

nzcAlignment: Nature of Science: Investigating in science, Understanding about science
Living World: Ecology - Explain how living things are suited to their particular habitat and how they respond to environmental changes
Planet Earth and Beyond: Earth systems - Investigate the water cycle and its dynamic effect on landscapes
NZC Key Competencies: Participating and contributing, Using language, symbols and texts
